item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The images used in this article are cc-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) as they were taken by the author.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Came for the spider, upvoted for the creativity. It was a little long and I felt some of the items became a little tedious but dude you have the framework for a potentially enormously popular universe that can be added to

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think the author took some random pics around his vacation home and wrote an SCP based on it.

Too much seemingly random elements thrown in without some sense of unification and some tone problems. A quick downvote from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There are quite a few moving parts here. They kind of seems to come together in the end, but not enough to make the long read worthwhile. In addition, the note at the end suffers from the stereotypical "note at the end" syndrome, and just seems to be there to tell rather than show.

No vote.

item.title: Wikiwalk Junior Staff Post
item.content:encoded:
edit: edits made

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team.

+ Show edits
- Hide

To link to other content featuring the mole rats, I propose the following edit:

We need to find this gateway and seal it for good, unless we've got the Brazen Balls to send the Mole Rats.

to

We need to find this gateway and seal it for good, unless we've got the Brazen Balls to send the Mole Rats.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Based on some of the things they've said it can do, we need to find it as soon as possible. I'm certain that it's a Keter of the worst sort.

That's a bit of a hasty assumption there doc.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This new "tell, don't show" addendum is an enormous step backward. You spend so much time focusing on these specific objects within the universe, but then you end it with someone telling us that, no, wait, actually this dimension is scary because of three things written on a note, none of which are the objects previously described, and none of which we get to see or have described to us. It completely undermines everything you've built.

Again, the reason this article is intriguing is the mystique of entering a weird sanitarium afterlife. And there's a lot of interesting stuff that goes with that. The problem is you try to shift the focus from all of that to making the universe a storage space for miscellaneous stuff, and that is what kills the article. You're essentially trying to write an article for an entire containment site instead of a specific SCP. I would remove all mention of "attics." Explaining the SCP removes the mystery.

But on the plus side, you did trim out two unnecessary objects. It's getting there.

As for the format, you seem really married to having individual object classifications, but there's still no logic to it. This is an alternate universe. Why would the Foundation categorize specific things in an alternate universe when the universe itself is anomalous?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Note taken, changes applied.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I still don't see the need for the overall Thaumiel classification and the individual classes for each sub-object. There are at least a dozen successful articles that have a big area with lots of different stuff in it and only need the one object class. This is probably, in total, Euclid, as -3 is contained in the overall thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hint taken. Giving this a serious overhaul.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That's a good indicator that this really needs to be reworked instead of gasping for air.

Edit: meant as a reply to above.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This scip really yo-yos the lowest rated section

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I watched this for a while before voting, its gotten a lot better and earned my upvote. Congrats at being out of the negatives.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Aw man this thing is back in the negatives. Shit :/

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
-1

Sorry, but I generally don't like GOIs, especially new ones. Also, as others have stated, this skip just has too much going on that is feels disconnected and disjointed. Unless I'm missing something major, there's not a lot of unity between them besides the fact that they're all owned by the same GOI.

It might work better as several separate skips, or if you reduced each item to a single sentence, but as it stands, it's just too cumbersome, and not very fun to read.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This scip is amazing. I wish I could give it a +2.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm glad you changed the object class, but it doesn't really feel like an overhaul.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Actually it's arguably worse now than it was. Your changes were:

1. Changing the new object class "Sammlung" into Thaumiel, which is wrong. The Foundation isn't using this universe as an anti-Keter. If anything, it would be Euclid, as are other, similar universes.

2. Removed Molok and the Brazen Heart. Which is probably for the best.

3. Adding an inventory of the contents of this universe. Not sure why you did that, or what that was trying to solve, but it's a step in the wrong direction. The addendums were the article's biggest problem, and instead of cutting them, you made them longer.

Meanwhile, the problems like the awkward format, the unnecessary elements, the mystery-killing tonal shift of the addendums, and the internal logic problem of why the Foundation is even sending its people into this world to perform experiments in the first place all still remain.

I applaud you displaying the willingness to rewrite, but I think there are some things you're taking as sacred cows with this article that you will need to discard.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: MAJOR OVERHAUL YO! Let's see if I can save this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I am guessing "Containment Procedures for an area that, while specifically contained, also contains objects with their own specific containment procedures."

Which is totally unnecessary because there are already at least half a dozen areas of this kind that get by fine with a single set of procedures under a conventional object class.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I meant what it means in the context of the article. -.-;

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like it and gave it a +1. That said, yeah, there are some problems:

Cut the custom object class and instead give it an overall class of either Safe or Euclid (depending on if you want there to be a real threat of breach or not). Remove -1 and everything above -6 (though I did quite like -7), remove the Brazen Heart stuff (although if you want to keep some kind of minor allusion somewhere that doesn't detract from the article then it could work). Cut the individual object classes for the sub-scips. There's definitely a good article in here, though, which is why I upvoted, and also because -3 was really creepy to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the idea of an Interdimensional Storage that some GOI could be using to keep their stuff in so others, like the Foundation, can't get to them too easily.
There's also a nice story in there about probably a place meant to judge and sentence members/enemies of this GOI.

However, both lumped together in the same area makes it a little disjointed.

I mean, I'd keep the house and its odd occupants, the 'trees' and the somewhat unneccesary pit of likes for one thing. And then the collection of further curio for another. And I say unneccesary because, why would you need a pit that immolates a liar when you've got some perfectly fine kitchen linoleum forcing them to tell the truth already?

There's one thing that I am curious about though…
Why does -6 needs to be protected from -7 specifically?
( And please do not tell me that -7 -8 -9 :| )

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ahh…I want to like this one, but I think it is very cluttered and made me feel a little jumbled without quite understanding what was going on in the end. I am not sure I understood the Sammlung classification either.

I don't want this to get downvoted to oblivion, because I think this is a very unique- if not sort of confusing- article. Luckily, there weren't any glaring tonal or grammar issues, which in my opinion is harder to fix than an idea.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Why are two of my favorites skips in the lower ranges? •A•

2sad4amu

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hesitantly downvoted. I see no purpose to the SCP other than "religious view on hate 'n' stuff" and it seems like a lot of half-fleshed out ideas thrown into a singular SCP. Too scatterbrained for me.

However, as far as I can tell, it is technically perfect, so good job for that! Also, I really like the usage of images here, been a while since we had an SCP that utilizes imagery to give a bit more feeling. I hope to see this expanded upon and the ideas linked together more. If you want my opinions on how to improve the article, shoot me a PM and I'll offer what I can.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There are quite a few large area SCPs with lots of loosely related parts that are able to get by fine with a normal object class. I am sure we could list at least half a dozen. SCP-1730 immediately stands out for me as of this kind.

item.title: Re: Upvote
item.content:encoded: I would think the most fitting designation would be Euclid. The alternate universe would definitely not be Thaumiel, and I wouldn't consider it to be Safe either.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wow. Dassa lotta controversy. Hope this stays 0 or above…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm going to have to expand on that, although I didn't read the article. I just came here to learn what "Sammlung" meant.

Show, because there's a lot of stuff
Aha! Click to collapse

Informally and extremely simplified,

Keter: Put it in a nearly indestructible box and hope/pray it doesn't kill us.

or more formally…

Keter-class objects are anomalies that pose an inimical threat to the safety of Foundation personnel and the rest of mankind and either require extensive and complex procedures to contain or cannot be fully contained by the Foundation's current technology and knowledge.

These anomalies are generally considered the most dangerous ones in Foundation containment, and all research efforts are directed towards enabling more reliable containment of such anomalies or, as a last resort, the timely neutralization or destruction of its anomalous effects.

Taken straight from the site's mouth…or text.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well yes, but you asked what Sammlung means, and I answered with what it means! :P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not the pertinent issue. It's a made-up, and therefore meaningless, object class. It's one more bad decision in an article that is comprised of bad decisions.

item.title: Upvote
item.content:encoded:
This was interesting. I got pulled in really fast in all the various anomalies, and both the addendum explained some of the story behind the SSCP.
A few points I think you should correct.

  • As mentioned before all the various anomalies distract from one another, I think that maybe if you found a way to separate them more so that each one will be showcased individually then it'll be better.
  • I really don't see why you need the "Sammlung" classification, Thaumiel or Safe will be more fitting.
  • I think that some of the anomalies should be classified as 'anomalous items' since they're not so important.

That's it, I guess. I don't know if this will succeed but even if it doesn't I hope that it'll find its way to the main site.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: German for "collection".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Tone is off in many spots ("disintegrate from within" as mentioned above, for one). What the hell is a "Sammlung"? This just seems like an excuse to publish a bunch of unrelated, underdeveloped, and generally uninteresting skips all at once.

item.title: nice
item.content:encoded: im to lazy to read it all but what i read it was cool upvote

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
keter=hard to contain
its hard to contain maybe

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Structure-wise, I get the feeling this article would probably be better organised in the same fashion as, say, SCP-1329, rather than a large number of collected mini-articles. It allows the ideas to fit together in an accumulative way rather than present a lot of separate ideas, most of which wouldn't be that interesting alone.

Additionally, I feel like there could be a bit more thematic cohesion to these things - there doesn't seem to be a clear rationale for why the clouds shoot cannonballs at you, or why that fire hydrant is radioactive.

All in all, it's rather scattershot.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the idea of expanding Moloch sub-mythos and inter-weaving it into the Brazen Heart, but I do not like this particular SCP. It does little to justify its unique object class, and each of these mind as well be sub-designations of an SCP under a standardised object class or separate anomalous objects/entities (like the Sarkic cult SCPs).

That said, I hope to see how you may develop your sub-mythos.

Addendum: Maybe it would do good to show how each of these items explore the aspects of the Brazen Heart.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't actively dislike this, but it feels like it would have been much better to write it as a single entry (there are many other anomalous areas with multiple related anomalies inside and they don't require this collective class) and most of the expository parts could be cut. There's some weird tonal issues (mainly with the parts that kill or injure): "disintegrate from within", "messily devour" and "immolation" (this in particular means "to offer up as a sacrifice" which nonlethal torture for interrogation probably isn't).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Perfectly fine start; bad end. The 'It was all cultists!' angle didn't really serve it, and just brings the pacing to a screeching halt. Everybody's read crazy cult hymns, and these ones aren't particularly special, nor is the group very anything. It just is. While there might not be much frighteningly unique about the first half, I'd say it'd work better with just 2-5. -7 is humorous, but would probably be a detriment to the tone.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think what Dave is trying to do with this Moloch/Molok stuff is basically what Meta did with the FLESH and Djoric did with the Scarlet King, with implications tying to both of those.

The writing here isn't incompetent, but it's not compelling. It uses a lot of well-established tropes and doesn't do anything new with them. To list a few, we have 1) an anomalous prophet/demigod/other servant of a god, 2) a place that exists in hammerspace (seriously, the Foundationverse is like, 90℅ hammerspace by volume), 3) a Strange Evil-but-justified-in-their-belief cult/cult that worships a Real Deity, 4) and an Ominous Countdown Timer. Those aren't even everything. These tropes can definitely still work (a lot of my favorites use some of these; I've used some of these) but they're not properly applied. I understand the idea that this is meant to be a sacred place for a religion you've probably mulled over a lot, that this is meant to be part of a larger mythos, but I don't feel like you presented it well. Your afterlife skip and the original Liar's Den are both pretty good at the Bigger Implications thing in my opinion, but this doesn't so much imply as rub our noses in it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Your new creepy cult is way too reliant on already established creepy cults to stand on it's own. The CotBG is written from plenty of other angles than crazy jerks, Fifthism is too weird to be labeled, and Sarkism is played straight, but with great detail and heavy lore. This Brazen Heart stuff has none of that, so I have no reason to care about them.

As for your new object class, I don't see the point, especially in this context. Why does anything else beyond making sure nobody reaches SCP-2427-A need to be done? The only mobile thing to worry about is SCP-2427-3, and on the chance it did find a sprig of hemlock and left, it would be contained like any other SCP that can't be looked at.

Overall, nothing adds up. I don't know why the foundation would send researchers to a dangerous extradimensional space for no reason, or why these Molok guys have such great taste in interior decorating.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The problem is this article is trying to do way too much. We have addendums that go on to explain what this universe is, when I thought the most interesting thing about it was that it's this weird, mysterious afterlife that happened to be a 1800s sanitarium. The intro of walking into an alternate reality with hemlock is a great start, filled with mystery, but this wonderful atmosphere is lost by explaining everything. Plus, you're already trying to create an entire dimension, but now you want to create an entire profile of a new GOI on top of that; and then on top of that you're creating a new object class; and then on top of that there's the unnecessary coda with business cards, which did nothing for me.

The article should have ended after -5. Personally, I would keep the stairs and -2 through -5, and remove all the others. They don't fit the narrative you're telling, some of them break tone (article seems to be going for weird, but then there's the Liar Pit which is gruesome, and the cloud which I thought was really funny), and their inclusion in this world that seems to focus on the soul-eating beast and body-eating tiles just bogs the rest down.

I'm not a fan of the format either. Contrast this with something like SCP-1165. To give each weird thing a separate designation makes us regard each thing as separate, and the result is disjointed. Make it all part of the same world and we take in the environment as a whole.

Please rewrite, because there's a good SCP in here. The problem is it's weighed down by too much excess.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My opinion on the piece still stands from the draft you posted on the forum. Nothing fits together at all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is GREAT!I love extradimensional stuff, and this was really well done!In particular, I liked how the stuff in -2 was like a judgement room.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I unketer'd -7.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
hrm, I liked portions of it, the -3 to -5 is interesting (Though 'messily devour' is pretty tone-breaking) But much of the rest of it feels like it's being weird for the sake of being weird and feels like they're just there to increase the object count and thus to justify the new class.

I also don't quite get why any of the objects here are Keter considering it's all locked up in what amounts to a pocket dimension.

The writing here also fails to really fail impart distinctiveness to the 'Brazen heart' that you're trying to promote. It honestly just feels like just another generically creepy/weird religion in the tradition of creepy/weird religions that crop up on the site.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Weird and cool. I like it!

+1

item.title: not bad
item.content:encoded: I like this SCP. its interesting and a good idea. but since im bad at critique, I cant say much. sorry

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Too many seemingly disparate elements that bog the article down and ultimately lead nowhere important. SCP-2427-2 is the only one that ultimately plays an important role by the end, and the rest just seem to be… there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What. The. Fuck.

That was gloriously weird and creepy. Upvote.

One thing though - I don't understand exactly how 2427-7 is a Keter…
